sentences.text: My name is Maurice Moore.
And I was born in Warsaw July 15, 1923.
M-A-U-R-I-C-E M-O-O-R-E.
My name in Poland was Moniek Miodownik.
That would be spelled M-O-N-I-E-K M-I-O-D-O-W-N-I-K.
No.
Now I'm 72 years old.
The slight confusion?
I have no idea.
I-- for some reason-- I don't know if I am right of-- or the records of the camp that I received just a few months ago, while I was there in-- in Auschwitz.
To be honest with you, after the war I just ideally said, I was born July 15, 1923, and I kept it on.
The record says that I was born in August of 1925.
I really-- I really don't remember.
I remember when I was a very little child, we used to live in Warsaw in a non-Jewish neighborhood.
When I reached school age we-- my father and mother sent me to a religious school-- a religious day school-- where we learned half a day English and half a day Hebrew.
By 1936, the conditions-- political conditions-- and anti-Semitism was so pronounced.
And it was almost dangerous to live in a non-Jewish neighborhood.
And we moved into a Jewish neighborhood, next to where my grandmother lived-- on Lewki 34.
I don't remember that age.
Most likely, yes.
Well, every morning we used to fight.
The Polish boys used to tell me that the Jewish boys killed their god.
And that's what the priest told them.
And as a result they were angry, although we grew up together and we were friends.
And all of a sudden things changed.
But they started going to school.
And we got stopped going to school.
Things changed entirely.
And we were no more close friends, although we were at times friends.
So we fought a little and we were friends a little.
And that's the time-- went by that way.
From the school, yeah.
But when I was like seven, eight, nine, 10 years old.
Oh, we were playing football.
You know, this was-- soccer was the big thing.
Every boy in Poland used to play soccer.
When I was a child?
It was a real wonderful school.
All the children were Jewish and the teachers were mixed.
And the Polish sessions we had Polish teachers, and-- and the Hebrew teachers, natural Jewish rabbis.
It wasn't like a modern Hebrew school that we know of now.
It was a Torah learning session-- how to read and interpret and learn Hebrew, [YIDDISH], how to-- how to pray, and the laws, and so forth.
That was the Hebrew education that we received.
Yeah.
These are faculty rabbis.
That was their profession.
They were professional teachers.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
At home we spoke Yiddish and Polish.
But my parents spoke Russian.
Because my mother went to school when the Russians occupied Poland.
So she was educated in Polish and Russian, and French and Yiddish and Hebrew.
So they were quite educated people.
My parents did.
Yeah.
Before the end of the First World War, Russia was in Poland.
Poland was not an independent country until 1918.
And my mother was born in 1900.
Learn in Poland, in school?
Just Yiddish, Polish, and part Hebrew.
I mean, a substantial amount of Hebrew.
Only Yiddish and Polish.
And my parents spoke Russian, only when they didn't want the children to understand.
But eventually we learned Russian, because it's very close to Polish.
Yeah, but he was a-- in-- in the jargon of Jewish view-- [NON-ENGLISH], which is a member of a certain group of people that go and support a certain rabbi.
And his rabbi was from a city called Kashmir.
So he was called a [NON-ENGLISH] Hasid.
And that rabbi-- that Kashmir rabbi used to live a few buildings from where we lived.
So we were always in walking distance to that particular rabbi.
And we prayed in his synagogue.
And we spent holidays there, in prayer.
And my father supported him just like all the other Hasidim supported this particular rabbi.
And every Jew that was a Hasid in Warsaw had his own personal rabbi.
Yeah, exactly.
A Hasidic Jew.
An orthodox Jew.
That's exactly what I mean.
I didn't have any problems, I was a young kid.
I went to this rabbi because my father went to him, and the children usually went to shul where their father went.
I'm not aware of it.
My mother was a very beautiful lady.
And she was very educated, very well educated.
And she was running the house.
And she was socially involved.
I'm not aware-- I'm not familiar what she did.
But she was active in various organizations.
And at that time I was too young to be aware of what the type of organizations she belonged to.
Probably organizations that are also, I would say, Hasidish leaning, on the very traditional Jewish way.
And almost everybody in Warsaw-- the majority of people were orthodox.
So she was also the same.
She was raised that way.
That's the way she was.
She went to the synagogue.
Well, it wasn't a synagogue.
It was like a house.
And the living room was a room that we did the prayers.
It was a larger living room and that's-- like a private shul.
I think he was a member of a Orthodox political party, which is called Agudath Israel.
And it's a very well-known organization.
It's still in existence today.
I was not.
I didn't belong to--
I'd say about 14.
While I was going to school I worked part-time in a-- a printing and rubber stamp company that was right-- our neighbors had the-- and I was working there.
I was delivering things.
I was doing-- go kind of chores.
And they used to give me $5 a week for part-time work.
And I-- it was handy money, and naturally I gave it to my mother because I didn't need any money.
My father-- I'll tell you-- most of the time he wasn't home.
He was in the fields.
His work required him to be outside the home.
He was buying fruits of the trees by ranchers.
And when the fruit ripens-- and he had people watching the ranches-- when the fruit ripens he organize it to bring it to Warsaw and sell it to wholesalers.
Friday was a busy day home.
My mother used to bake challahs, make gefilte fish.
Everything was made from scratch.
It was like a holiday.
Yeah, very, very important.
Everything was made home.
The cakes, the fish, the challah, the chicken-- whatever she made was home-cooked.
Well, I'll tell you what.

The situation in Poland was so that almost every Jewish young man, being aware of situation that we lived at the time, was-- our dream was to go either to Palestine, at the time, or to the United States.
Almost everyone had some member of his family in America.
And we had very good news that in America you can live a freer life.
A life of equality.
And you don't have to worry about being Jewish, or being beaten up if you go in a neighborhood other than a Jewish neighborhood.
And, the-- also the economic situation in Poland was not exactly the best.
So Jewish boys usually dreamed of going to America.
And that included me.
No, my father had a cousin in America.
So that gave us a little bit of an idea-- in New York.
We knew about trouble in Germany when I was 10 years old.
And there were always things happen to Germany, because a lot of Jewish people from Poland used to emigrate to Germany in order to get jobs there, or be in business there.
We moved to the Jewish neighborhoods.
Yeah, it was very bad.
Yeah, sure.
No, it was just the Jewish neighborhood, just like here would be Fairfax.
Yeah.
We just left one apartment.
In Poland, you used to buy apartments.
You used to sell an apartment and buy an apartment.
It wasn't really your property but it was like rent control, you know.
Like, if you pay-- let's see-- $200 a month, whoever you sold them to also paid $200 a month.
You know, most of the people that we knew, we were praying for Mashiyah to come.
But, practically, people were really concerned.
And the Polacks, they acted as if they would be the Germans.
So we were afraid.
Yeah, because when a Jewish person-- especially when he was bearded and he had the outfit of a religious Jew-- went in a non-Jewish neighborhood, he was attacked.
His beard was shorn off.
He was beaten up by hooligans.
I mean, not 100% of the Polish population acted that way, but a great-- a lot of people were anti-Semitic.
Jewish businesses-- now, the business owners [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] weren't-- they were the owners of the businesses, but the people that worked for them were non-Jews.
They run the businesses in the non-Jewish neighborhoods.
And certain-- and not all the Jews in Poland-- in Warsaw-- were Hasidim.
They were very much Westernized.
The business people were a different group of people, especially people in the retail business, people in banking, and insurances.
They were entirely different.
A different group of people.
They wore modern Western clothing.
They were shaved.
They wore shirt and ties.
They were just like Americans now.
There was the-- they were a different class of people.
Not everybody was Hasidish, or wore a beard in Warsaw.
They couldn't be recognized so easily.
A man-- a Jewish person that had a beard, and he had these dark black clothes on-- he was easily identified.
So he was beaten up.
It wasn't like a-- a revolt against the Jews, but almost every day some Jewish people in Warsaw in certain neighborhoods were attacked because they were Jewish.
Oh, Western.
I-- I didn't want that.
I was dressed like now.
September 1, 1939, was the first day the Germans attacked Poland.
And planes were over Warsaw with scattered bombardment, not very heavy.
And there was the mobilization right away.
And Germany started marching into Poland.
And they marched all over Poland in, maybe, eight days.
They were on the outskirts of the city of Warsaw.
And then the military decided that they will defend the city of Warsaw.
And the German encircled the city of Warsaw with artillery, and were shooting down into the city.
And they were shooting down into the city.
That was the third week of September in 1939.
And that where my tragedy happened.
Yeah.
Well, there was an artillery shell, fell into the building that we lived in on the first floor, sending down the floor completely into the basement.
And all my family, and many other neighbors, were in the basement as a-- in the-- at night, because they told us to go into the basement because they were bombarding it from airplanes.
But the bombardment was not from airplanes.
They were from artillery.
And they sent down the floor, and as a result my parents, and one my brothers, plus-- including many members of my family, totalling 17 people of my family-- got killed in that particular basement.
But my grandmother that was also in that basement was not killed.
And the reason was-- she was in a pocket where she could get air.
The most of the people died because of lack of air.
They suffocated.
And my brother and I were in the third floor of the building, that where we lived.
And we didn't even know that because there was no explosion.
So when we got down early in the morning, about 6 o'clock, the neighbors that were not affected did not want to believe that we were not with our parents, and told us what happened.
And we were in charge of digging out the people from the basement.
And we were putting them all on the-- on the yard-- on the courtyard of the building.
And two of my uncles survived, my mother's two brothers.
And my brother and I.
But four men.
And each member of the family had to bury their own members of the family that got killed in that particular basement.
And it happens-- that happens in many basements in the city of Warsaw.
Not only in the Jewish neighborhoods but in the non-Jewish neighborhoods, as well.
So they weren't killed because they were Jews, it's just a unfortunate-- fortune of wars.
Well, the artillery came this way.
You know?
No, no.
Artillery-- all around Warsaw, there was-- encircled the city of Warsaw-- and the artillery were shooting down into the city just everywhere.
And we had to bury our own dead, because at that time nobody wanted to accept Polish money.
Even if you had the money you couldn't get anybody to work for it.
So we had to take our own cadavers-- members-- and put them on pushcarts.
And taking the pushcart to the Warsaw cemetery-- Jewish cemetery-- digging a common grave.
And we dug a grave for 37 people from our building.
And we buried them according to the Jewish law, as a group in their clothes.
And that was the first experience that I had in the war of a personal nature.
Well, we were frozen.
My mind wasn't functioning.
I had just to do things because this was the things to do.
There was no past.
There was no future.
There was just the present.
What do we do now?
After we buried our dead we were sitting shiva according to the Jewish customs.
And the whole building was sitting shiva.
And it was a tragedy, enormous proportions.
We lost our most important people.
And my-- thank God, Grandma was alive.
So she took care of us, my brother and I.
And she cooked for us, she baked for us.
She took-- she was a mother all over again.
And this is what happened in September of 1939.
Did the rabbi that your father followed survive that bombardment?
Did you--
I personally didn't meet him after that day.
I did not go there.
I was very much hurt because we always were thinking that good people that do good deeds and good things should happen to them.
And I wouldn't consider that as a good thing that happened to me.
I really almost denounced the religiosity.
I wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] any more.
I mean, I was observing because of my father.
But we had to say Kaddish every day because that-- that was the custom.
So we had to daven and say Kaddish for 11 months.
We didn't need a rabbi.
Everybody-- every Jew can conduct services.
During that time there was a question of, we have to eat.
Somebody has to support us.
And there was no-- money ran out, whatever little money we had.
And all the things-- like food and whatever people need for everyday survival, got more expensive.
Inflation sets in.
And the woman that owned the printing and rubber stamp company offered me a partnership.
Because I knew how to make rubber stamps.
And people used to come from all over to buy rubber stamps of every description.
And so I started making money because we started charging prices-- high prices-- because we couldn't replace the raw materials.
They weren't available.
So this is what made me money to survive in the ghetto-- and right after the tragedy that happened.
